title: Concomitant Predictors and Distal Outcomes
text: mixLCA enforces temporal separation three blocks variables: Antecedent concomitant predictors: variables come class formation shift probability belonging latent class (e.g., demographics). Contemporaneous manifest indicators: variables define classes (measurement model). Subsequent distal outcomes: variables consequences class membership contaminate . Mixing blocks naively produces double-counted information. Letting distal outcome help define classes mechanically inflates predictive power; letting concomitant predictor enter measurement model leaks variance class meaning. mixLCA keeps three blocks separate: concomitants enter multinomial logistic regression class membership, distals estimated measurement model converged, Bollen-Curran-Heckman (BCH) inverse-classification-error weighting. vignette walks pieces Pima Indians Diabetes dataset, small, well-known mixed dataset continuous health indicators, age covariate, binary diabetes outcome.

title: Concomitant Predictors and Distal Outcomes
text: diabetes status consequence metabolic class. put measurement model; estimate separately BCH weighting. BCH method (Bolck, Croon, Hagenaars 2004; refined Vermunt 2010) constructs class-specific inverse-classification-error weights fitted posteriors, fits class-specific regression distal outcome using weights. measurement model frozen distal step, gradient distal outcome can contaminate class meaning. Reading output: class 1 (metabolically healthier class) negative diabetes intercept (low baseline risk); class 2 positive intercept (higher baseline risk). age slope within class small. Class membership already absorbs age signal. classification error matrix bottom tells confident modal assignment . Diagonal dominance close 1 means BCH adjustment mild; values closer 0.5 mean classification uncertain BCH weights pulling strongly.
code: pima_distal <- distal(pima_concom_chr, pima,                       formula = diabetes ~ age,                       family  = "binomial") print(pima_distal) #>  #> Distal Outcome Estimation (BCH Method) - mixLCA #> ================================================ #> Formula: diabetes ~ age  #> Family : binomial  #> Classes: 2  #>  #> --- Class 1 --- #>             Estimate       SE      z     p #> (Intercept) -1.39513  2.54052 -0.549 0.583 #> age         -0.02859  0.10220 -0.280 0.780 #>   Effective N: 373.5  #>  #> --- Class 2 --- #>             Estimate       SE      z      p   #> (Intercept)  0.85883  0.47377  1.813 0.0699 . #> age         -0.01126  0.01066 -1.056 0.2908   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>   Effective N: 350.5  #>  #> Classification Error Matrix: #>        [,1]  [,2] #> [1,] 0.9171 0.164 #> [2,] 0.0829 0.836

title: Introduction to mixLCA
text: mixLCA fits finite mixture models latent class analysis partitioned architecture: concomitant predictors enter multinomial logistic regression class membership; manifest indicators (continuous, categorical, mixed) define measurement model; distal outcomes estimated measurement model converges, Bollen-Croon-Hagenaars inverse-classification-error weighting. partition prevents distal outcomes leaking class meaning concomitants contaminating measurement structure. package supports two responses local dependence among manifest indicators: Direct effects categorical items (Hagenaars-Vermunt specification search via auto_bvr()). Spectral Local Dependence (SLD) categorical items: low-rank decomposition conditional Burt matrix (auto_sld()). continuous indicators, covariance structure can fully unrestricted, diagonal, sparsified via graphical lasso (dependence = "penalized").

title: Introduction to mixLCA
code: library(mixLCA) data("PimaIndiansDiabetes2", package = "mlbench") pima <- na.omit(PimaIndiansDiabetes2)  fit <- fit_lca(pima,                continuous  = c("glucose","pressure","mass","pedigree"),                concomitant = ~ age,                n_classes   = 2,                control     = lca_control(n_starts = 10, seed = 110))  summary(fit, data = pima) plot(fit, type = "profiles")  distal(fit, pima, diabetes ~ age, family = "binomial")

title: Introduction to mixLCA
text: Core fitting: fit_lca(), lca_control(): entry point optimiser settings. auto_bvr(): BVR-guided specification search. auto_sld(): adaptive Spectral Local Dependence rank selection. enumerate_lca(): fit range KK values. Diagnostics: fit_indices(): AIC, BIC, aBIC, entropy, ICL. bvr_tests(), bvr_categorical(): bivariate residual diagnostics. class_table(): modal-class x posterior cross-tabulation. Inference: predict() (S3 method): posteriors, modal class, full diagnostic frame. distal(): BCH-weighted regression distal outcomes. concomitant_se(), continuous_se(): asymptotic standard errors. get_posteriors(), get_loadings(), coef(): accessors. Visualisation: plot() (S3 method) type = c("profiles", "bvr", "distal",   "uncertainty", "convergence", "categorical", "spectral_scree",   "spectral_loadings").

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example
text: local-independence assumption underwrites every classical LCA: given class kk, manifest indicators Y1,…,YJY_1, \ldots, Y_J statistically independent. practice assumption frequently fails. Two established responses exist. Direct effects (Hagenaars 1988; Vermunt 1999) add conditional probability table P(Yj∣Yj′,C=k)P(Y_j \mid Y_{j'}, C = k) one item pairs (j,j′)(j, j'). remedy targeted cheap local dependence sits small number identifiable pairs, scales O(J2)O(J^2) candidate pairs search produces dense parameter tables pair admitted. Latent trait extensions (Magidson Vermunt’s mixed mixture models, Bartolucci’s hidden-Markov LCA, factor-mixture models) add within-class continuous latent variable. handle distributed dependence break discrete-class story introduce numerical integration. Spectral Local Dependence (SLD) sits two. introduces rank-dkd_klow-dimensional shift class-conditional log response surface. shift sufficient capture broad correlated residuals, leaves class labels discrete M-step closed-form (eigendecomposition). vignette gives math, identification argument, implementation mixLCA, worked example.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example
text: Let JJ number categorical items, item jj taking values {1,…,Kj}\{1, \ldots, K_j\}. Stack one-hot encoding items single binary vector 𝐙i∈{0,1}C,C:=∑j=1JKj. \mathbf{Z}_i \\{0,1\}^C, \qquad C := \sum_{j=1}^J K_j.  respondent ii, exactly one entry item’s KjK_j-block 𝐙i\mathbf{Z}_i equals 1. Let 𝛑(k)∈[0,1]C\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} \[0,1]^C class-kk marginal response probabilities (standard LCA parameters), one block per item summing 1. local independence, row-wise expectation 𝐙\mathbf{Z} conditional class kk 𝛑(k)\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} conditional variance known multinomial form. Define class-conditional residual 𝐑i(k):=𝐙i−𝛑(k). \mathbf{R}^{(k)}_i := \mathbf{Z}_i - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)}.  second moment class conditional Burt matrix 𝚺(k):=𝔼[𝐑(k)𝐑(k)⊤∣C=k]. \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{(k)} :=   \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbf{R}^{(k)}\mathbf{R}^{(k)\top} \mid C = k\right].  local independence, -block-diagonal entries 𝚺(k)\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{(k)} zero: items uncorrelated given class. Local dependence shows nonzero -block-diagonal entries.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example

Let 𝛈i(k):=log𝛑(k)\boldsymbol{\eta}^{(k)}_i := \log \boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} local-independence log-probability vector. SLD adds hollow projection vector: 𝛈i(k):=log𝛑(k)+𝐀⋆(k)𝐑i(k),𝐀⋆(k):=𝐌⊙(𝐕(k)𝐕(k)⊤), \boldsymbol{\eta}^{(k)}_i := \log \boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)}   + \mathbf{}^{\star (k)} \mathbf{R}^{(k)}_i, \qquad \mathbf{}^{\star (k)} := \mathbf{M} \odot \left(   \mathbf{V}^{(k)} \mathbf{V}^{(k)\top} \right),  𝐌\mathbf{M} C×CC \times C “hollow” mask (1 -block-diagonal, 0 -block-diagonal) prevents projection touching within-item part parameters. within-item part already parameterized 𝛑(k)\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} double-counting break identification. log-density per item block softmax shifted 𝛈i(k)\boldsymbol{\eta}^{(k)}_i restricted item’s columns. composite log-likelihood sum across blocks. Note: 𝐀⋆(k)\mathbf{}^{\star (k)} symmetric rank-dkd_k (masking; mask preserves rank numerical tolerance). identification 𝐕(k)\mathbf{V}^{(k)} orthogonal rotation within column span, indeterminacy factor analysis correspondence analysis.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example
text: EM one twist: E-step. Compute posteriors γik∝πk⋅softmaxJ(𝛈i(k))\gamma_{ik} \propto \pi_k \cdot \mathrm{softmax}_J(\boldsymbol{\eta}^{(k)}_i), usual. M-step. class kk: Update marginal response probabilities 𝛑(k)\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} weighted posterior counts (standard LCA M-step). Form weighted conditional residual covariance 𝚺̂(k)=1∑iγik∑iγik𝐑i(k)𝐑i(k)⊤, \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{(k)} =   \frac{1}{\sum_i \gamma_{ik}}   \sum_i \gamma_{ik} \, \mathbf{R}^{(k)}_i \mathbf{R}^{(k)\top}_i,  𝐑i(k)=𝐙i−𝛑(k)\mathbf{R}^{(k)}_i = \mathbf{Z}_i - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)}. Compute eigendecomposition 𝐌⊙𝚺̂(k)\mathbf{M} \odot \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{(k)}, keep top dkd_k eigenvectors, form 𝐀⋆(k)=𝐌⊙𝐕(k)𝐕(k)⊤\mathbf{}^{\star (k)} = \mathbf{M} \odot \mathbf{V}^{(k)} \mathbf{V}^{(k)\top}. Use 𝐀⋆(k)\mathbf{}^{\star (k)} construct shifted log-density next E-step. M-step closed-form (eigendecomposition), per-iteration cost O(K⋅C3)O(K \cdot C^3), dominated eigendecomposition, CC rarely exceeding dozen cheap. EM monotonicity scheme rests generalized M-step argument: eigendecomposition selects best rank-dkd_k projection Frobenius norm, also rank-dkd_k projection maximises Q-function locally. mixLCA adds step-halving safeguard R/04_em_engine.R backs candidate update fails non-decrease observed log-likelihood; practice safeguard fires rarely.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example
text: Three strategies. Fixed rank. Pass spectral_rank = 2L (scalar, rank classes) spectral_rank = c(1L, 2L, 0L) (length-KK vector). Pooled basis. Set spectral_pool = TRUE estimate single spectral basis class-weighted average conditional Burt matrices, applied uniformly every class. sacrifices class- specific dependence structure sharper estimate dominant directions, useful sample size per class small. Adaptive search. auto_sld() runs greedy forward search: step identifies class largest unmodelled eigenvalue, tries adding one rank , accepts BIC (AIC, aBIC, ICL) improves. search stops class can improve rank cap reached. result class-specific ranks matched data’s actual residual structure.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example

Without spectral shift absorb information already encoded 𝛑(k)\boldsymbol{\pi}^{(k)} (within-item probabilities), producing non-identified surplus. M-step composite (maximise sum marginal item softmaxes, full multinomial joint), BIC computed usual way naive: -penalises composite likelihood. rank selection publication settings, prefer cross-validation BIC. mixLCA flags summary() output spectral_rank > 0.

title: Spectral Local Dependence: Theory and Worked Example
text: election dataset used vignette("workflow") dominated pairwise dependence, direct effects win . SLD wins local dependence broad. small synthetic example illustrates. generate N=500N = 500 observations two classes, 8 binary items. class 1, items 1-4 positively correlated via shared latent factor; class 2 items 5-8 positively correlated via different shared factor. Direct effects need enumerate (42)⋅2=12\binom{4}{2} \cdot 2 = 12 pairs absorb structure; SLD captures two rank-1 projections. SLD model captures within-class correlation one rank per class gains BIC despite extra parameters (naive BIC ≈5350\approx 5350 vs. SLD BIC ≈5188\approx 5188, savings 160 points). Inspecting loadings shows rank-1 direction class 1 bipolar contrast: items Q1-Q4 (items whose latent factor active class 1) load one sign, items Q5-Q8 load opposite sign. single dimension thereby encodes covariance pattern induced shared latent factor:
code: sim_one_class <- function(n, p_base, latent_loading) {   # latent ~ N(0,1); item j response is 1 with prob expit(logit(p_base[j]) + latent_loading[j] * latent)   L <- rnorm(n)   out <- sapply(seq_along(p_base), function(j) {     eta <- stats::qlogis(p_base[j]) + latent_loading[j] * L     rbinom(n, 1, plogis(eta))   })   as.data.frame(out) }  withr::with_seed(110, {   c1 <- sim_one_class(250,     p_base = c(0.7,0.7,0.7,0.7, 0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3),     latent_loading = c(2,2,2,2, 0,0,0,0))   c2 <- sim_one_class(250,     p_base = c(0.3,0.3,0.3,0.3, 0.7,0.7,0.7,0.7),     latent_loading = c(0,0,0,0, 2,2,2,2))   df_sim <- rbind(c1, c2) }) df_sim[] <- lapply(df_sim, function(x) factor(x, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("no","yes"))) names(df_sim) <- paste0("Q", 1:8) str(df_sim) #> 'data.frame':    500 obs. of  8 variables: #>  $ Q1: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 1 2 2 2 2 2 1 2 1 2 ... #>  $ Q2: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 1 2 ... #>  $ Q3: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 2 2 1 2 2 2 1 2 1 2 ... #>  $ Q4: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 2 2 2 1 2 2 2 2 1 2 ... #>  $ Q5: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 1 2 2 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 ... #>  $ Q6: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 1 1 1 2 1 1 1 1 2 1 ... #>  $ Q7: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 2 1 1 1 2 1 2 2 1 2 ... #>  $ Q8: Factor w/ 2 levels "no","yes": 1 1 1 1 1 2 1 1 2 1 ... # Naive K=2 fit_naive_sim <- fit_lca(df_sim,                          categorical = names(df_sim),                          n_classes = 2,                          control = lca_control(n_starts = 5, seed = 110),                          verbose = FALSE)  # SLD K=2 with rank 1 per class fit_sld_sim <- fit_lca(df_sim,                        categorical = names(df_sim),                        n_classes = 2,                        spectral_rank = c(1L, 1L),                        control = lca_control(n_starts = 5, seed = 110),                        verbose = FALSE)  data.frame(   Model    = c("Naive", "SLD (rank 1 each)"),   log_lik  = round(c(fit_naive_sim$log_lik, fit_sld_sim$log_lik), 2),   n_params = c(fit_naive_sim$n_params, fit_sld_sim$n_params),   BIC      = round(c(fit_indices(fit_naive_sim)$BIC,                      fit_indices(fit_sld_sim)$BIC), 2) ) #>               Model  log_lik n_params     BIC #> 1             Naive -2622.30       17 5350.25 #> 2 SLD (rank 1 each) -2497.49       31 5187.63 get_loadings(fit_sld_sim)[   get_loadings(fit_sld_sim)$class == "Class 1" &     get_loadings(fit_sld_sim)$dimension == 1, ] #>      class dimension item category     loading #> 1  Class 1         1   Q1       no  0.21629277 #> 2  Class 1         1   Q1      yes -0.21629277 #> 3  Class 1         1   Q2       no  0.33012727 #> 4  Class 1         1   Q2      yes -0.33012727 #> 5  Class 1         1   Q3       no  0.24362195 #> 6  Class 1         1   Q3      yes -0.24362195 #> 7  Class 1         1   Q4       no  0.24315168 #> 8  Class 1         1   Q4      yes -0.24315168 #> 9  Class 1         1   Q5       no -0.02299066 #> 10 Class 1         1   Q5      yes  0.02299066 #> 11 Class 1         1   Q6       no -0.28537661 #> 12 Class 1         1   Q6      yes  0.28537661 #> 13 Class 1         1   Q7       no -0.30480933 #> 14 Class 1         1   Q7      yes  0.30480933 #> 15 Class 1         1   Q8       no -0.22557022 #> 16 Class 1         1   Q8      yes  0.22557022

title: A Workflow for Latent Class Analysis: Naive Fit, BVR Adjustments, and Spectral Local Dependence
text: Latent class models easy -trust. default specification assumes manifest indicators locally independent within class. , conditional class membership items carry information . assumption violated, EM algorithm compensates inventing extra classes whose purpose absorb pairwise correlation. resulting solution inflates KK, distorts class meaning, inflates standard errors downstream distal analysis. vignette walks three responses problem single dataset: naive fit treats local independence given. BVR-guided fit adds pairwise direct effects manifest items whose bivariate residuals (Vermunt, 1999) exceed chi-squared threshold. fit Spectral Local Dependence (SLD), low-rank decomposition conditional Burt matrix captures correlated structure across many items (see companion vignette vignette("sld-theory") math). use poLCA::election, study respondents rated moral character, competence, dishonesty George W. Bush (G items) Al Gore (B items) 2000. items come conceptually parallel pairs (MORALG/MORALB, KNOWG/KNOWB, ), priori reason expect local dependence: ratings candidate cluster together, parallel items two candidates may also covary partisan affect.

title: A Workflow for Latent Class Analysis: Naive Fit, BVR Adjustments, and Spectral Local Dependence
text: auto_bvr() automates Hagenaars-Vermunt-Magidson specification search: step identifies largest BVR pair, adds direct effect, refits, stops BIC ceases improve (cap hit). search added three direct effects: three involve KNOW items, interpretable: judgements much candidate “knows” cluster judgements competence (INTELG, LEADB) candidate. BVR machinery surfaced already visible residual table. BIC drops sharply (~ 33 points per direct effect dataset, far excess BIC penalty extra parameters). specification straightforwardly better, without inflating KK.
code: elec_bvr <- auto_bvr(   data = elec, categorical = cat_items,   K_range = 3,                 # fix K so the comparison stays clean   max_direct_effects = 4L,   bvr_threshold = 3.84,   seed = 110, verbose = FALSE,   n_starts = 5)  elec_bvr$auto_path$direct_effects elec_bvr$auto_path$direct_effects #> [[1]] #> [1] "KNOWG"  "INTELG" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "KNOWB"  "INTELB" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "KNOWB" "LEADB" fi_naive <- fit_indices(fit_K3) fi_bvr   <- fit_indices(elec_bvr)  data.frame(   Model      = c("Naive K=3", "BVR-adjusted K=3"),   log_lik    = round(c(fi_naive$log_lik, fi_bvr$log_lik), 2),   n_params   = c(fi_naive$n_params,     fi_bvr$n_params),   BIC        = round(c(fi_naive$BIC,    fi_bvr$BIC), 2),   entropy    = round(c(fi_naive$entropy, fi_bvr$entropy), 3) ) #>              Model   log_lik n_params      BIC entropy #> 1        Naive K=3 -16714.66      110 34218.96   0.834 #> 2 BVR-adjusted K=3 -15957.32      191 33285.75   0.819

title: A Workflow for Latent Class Analysis: Naive Fit, BVR Adjustments, and Spectral Local Dependence
text: Direct effects work pair pair. dependence low-dimensional (example, underlying latent direction partisanship pulls many ratings candidate together), SLD captures structure single rank-dd projection instead d⋅Kd \cdot K separate pairwise terms. auto_sld() runs greedy forward search class-specific ranks, adding one rank class largest unmodelled eigenvalue, accepting BIC improves. auto_sld settles ranks [1, 0, 2]: class 2 (moderate/ambivalent class) gets spectral structure, two polarized classes pick least one low-rank latent direction. BIC drops ~34219 (naive K=3) ~33763, real gain, though smaller BVR-adjusted fit (~33286). comparison informative : dataset, pairwise direct effects win. election items divide cleanly G-pairs B-pairs, local dependence shows small number large pairwise residuals, exactly direct effects designed absorb. SLD captures structure spends parameters every item-pair coupling latent direction implies, even pairs data flag dependent. SLD shines elsewhere: residual covariance broadly distributed across many items (say, partisanship dimension shifts every rating slightly), rank-dd projection beats (J2)\binom{J}{2} candidate direct-effect terms. See vignette("sld-theory") math worked example. can also inspect SLD loadings read items contribute latent direction: SLD loadings: rank 1 class 3.
code: elec_sld <- auto_sld(   data = elec, categorical = cat_items,   n_classes = 3,   max_rank_per_class = 3L,   criterion = "BIC",   seed = 110, verbose = FALSE) elec_sld$specs$spectral_rank elec_sld$specs$spectral_rank #> [1] 1 0 2 round(fit_indices(elec_sld)$BIC, 2) #> [1] 33762.96 fi_naive <- fit_indices(fit_K3) fi_bvr   <- fit_indices(elec_bvr) fi_sld   <- fit_indices(elec_sld)  data.frame(   Model    = c("Naive K=3", "BVR (3 direct effects)", "SLD (ranks 1,0,2)"),   n_params = c(fi_naive$n_params, fi_bvr$n_params, fi_sld$n_params),   BIC      = round(c(fi_naive$BIC, fi_bvr$BIC, fi_sld$BIC), 2),   entropy  = round(c(fi_naive$entropy, fi_bvr$entropy, fi_sld$entropy), 3) ) #>                    Model n_params      BIC entropy #> 1              Naive K=3      110 34218.96   0.834 #> 2 BVR (3 direct effects)      191 33285.75   0.819 #> 3      SLD (ranks 1,0,2)      213 33762.96   0.819 plot(elec_sld, type = "spectral_loadings", dimension = 1, class = 3)

what: 5. Substantive comparison: who are the classes?
title: A Workflow for Latent Class Analysis: Naive Fit, BVR Adjustments, and Spectral Local Dependence
text: class proportions move slightly. substantive class meaning changes visibly response profiles: Naive K=3: P(rating = ‘Extremely well’ | class) six G-items. three classes separate expect: one strongly positive Bush across items, one negative, one ambivalent. BVR- adjusted model recovers broad story sharper edges direct effects relieve latent classes explaining within-candidate covariance: BVR-adjusted K=3: items.
code: props_naive <- round(colMeans(get_posteriors(fit_K3)), 3) props_bvr   <- round(colMeans(get_posteriors(elec_bvr)), 3) data.frame(class = 1:3, naive = props_naive, bvr_adjusted = props_bvr) #>   class naive bvr_adjusted #> 1     1 0.419        0.419 #> 2     2 0.320        0.260 #> 3     3 0.261        0.321 # Build a small profile plot manually from the categorical params. # This keeps the vignette dependency-light and shows readers exactly # how to extract any quantity from a fitted mixLCA object. extract_extremely_well <- function(fit, items) {   K <- fit$n_classes   cp <- fit$categorical_params   out <- matrix(NA_real_, K, length(items), dimnames = list(paste("Class", 1:K), items))   for (k in 1:K) {     for (it in items) {       probs <- cp[[k]][[it]]       hit   <- grep("Extremely well", names(probs), ignore.case = TRUE, fixed = FALSE)       if (length(hit)) out[k, it] <- probs[[hit[1]]]     }   }   out }  g_items <- c("MORALG","CARESG","KNOWG","LEADG","DISHONG","INTELG") mat_naive <- extract_extremely_well(fit_K3, g_items)  matplot(t(mat_naive), type = "b", pch = 19, lty = 1,         xaxt = "n", xlab = "", ylab = "P(rating = 'Extremely well')",         main = "Naive K=3, items about Bush") axis(1, at = seq_along(g_items), labels = g_items, las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8) legend("topright", legend = rownames(mat_naive), pch = 19, col = 1:nrow(mat_naive), bty = "n") mat_bvr <- extract_extremely_well(elec_bvr, g_items) matplot(t(mat_bvr), type = "b", pch = 19, lty = 1,         xaxt = "n", xlab = "", ylab = "P(rating = 'Extremely well')",         main = "BVR-adjusted K=3, items about Bush") axis(1, at = seq_along(g_items), labels = g_items, las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8) legend("topright", legend = rownames(mat_bvr), pch = 19, col = 1:nrow(mat_bvr), bty = "n")

Pairwise vs. low-rank. Direct effects handle misbehaving pairs efficiently; SLD handles broad residual structure efficiently. two complementary; fit_lca() lets supply . Don’t add classes absorb dependence. naive K=4 fit looked like winning BIC. model local dependence properly, K=3 fine, class meaning preserved across refits. title: Partitioned Latent Class Analysis with Local Dependence and Distal Outcomes
text: Mixed indicators. Continuous (multivariate normal optional graphical-lasso penalisation) categorical (product-multinomial), jointly alone. Partitioned architecture. Antecedent concomitant predictors enter via multinomial logistic regression class membership; contemporaneous manifest indicators define measurement model; subsequent distal outcomes estimated separately Bollen-Croon-Hagenaars (BCH) inverse-classification-error weighting. Local dependence. Direct effects categorical pairs (Hagenaars-Vermunt specification search via auto_bvr()) Spectral Local Dependence (auto_sld()), rank-d eigendecomposition conditional Burt matrix. Honest standard errors. Full-Hessian SEs continuous parameters, sandwich SEs NA--failure distal regressions.

text: Three-phase automated model selection: Phase 1 Select K BIC across K_range using     diagonal covariance.
